text: The surgeon begins the dissection from the right common iliac artery towards the duodenum, following the aorta. The peritoneum is opened to perform a medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon locates the left ureter before proceeding with the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).

The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using a Hem-o-lok clip, performing a dissection that accommodates the clip placement. The dissection is done in front of the hypogastric nerves, which are carefully preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the duodenum or pancreas, followed by mobilization of the splenic flexure, a common step in rectal surgeries.
The surgeon separates the mesentery from the Gerota's fascia, continuing the dissection laterally to the edge of the Toldt's fascia and vertically to the lower end of the pancreas.
The surgeon initiates upper mesodissection by separating the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures and facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon dissects the pancreatic or colonic peritoneum using a lateral to medial approach, carefully cutting through the tissue layers.
The surgeon begins cutting the taut fascia at the level of the descending colon, aiming to reach the marker gas sponge placed earlier in the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the gas sponge marker, confirming the correct dissection plane, and successfully mobilizes the splenic flexure with precision.

The surgeon plans to dissect ten centimeters distal to the upper rectum tumor, cutting the peritoneum in the medulla of placation. The dissection begins posteriorly, with careful protection of the hypogastric nerves, and continues from the right lateral and anterior aspects.
The surgeon initiates left dissection by opening the peritoneum, ensuring the left ureter is visible to avoid common ureter injuries during this phase of the procedure.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler to transect the middle rectum at the ten-centimeter mark from the tumor, aiming to complete the transection with a single stapler, although multiple staplers may be necessary.
The surgeon uses a green stapler to complete the transection of the rectum, as the first stapler was insufficient to fully transect the tissue.
The surgeon identifies areas where the B formation is inadequate and applies V-lock sutures for reinforcement, visibly securing the tissue.

The surgeon performs a rectosigmoid resection, excising the upper border of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to the mesocolon through a fine steel incision measuring seven to eight centimeters. A 31-millimeter wide anvil is placed into the colon.
The surgeon washes out the distal rectum with povidone-iodine solution, preparing the area for the placement of a circular stapler.
The surgeon inspects the mesentery for free movement and rotation, ensuring no tissue is trapped in the stapler. An air leak test is performed to evaluate the integrity of the anastomosis.
